turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Discussing Study or Career Plans with an Advisor Scenario: You are speaking with an academic advisor or career counsellor to get guidance about your current study plans or future career options. You want to explain your situation, ask for advice, and clarify next steps. Practise speaking clearly and professionally in a formal setting. You will need to define the chatbot’s role as a university advisor or career counsellor. You can choose situations from the examples below; start with a basic question about your study plan, and try a more extended interaction by exploring course structures, comparing qualifications, or evaluating career outcomes. Learning objectives: • Practise describing academic or professional goals. • Use formal and polite question forms. • Respond to suggestions and express uncertainty or preferences. • (Advanced option) Manage a longer discussion by comparing study pathways, discussing industry requirements, or asking for help with future planning. Instructions: Try to: • Explain your current situation (what you study or do). • Ask about career pathways or study options. • Respond to advice and ask follow-up questions. • Summarise your next steps politely. • (Advanced option) Use the extension prompts below to have a longer conversation

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Alright. I’m your university academic advisor—could you briefly tell me what you’re currently studying or working in and what prompted you to seek advice today?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “Hey, I'm studying business, but I'm not sure it's the right fit.”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Alright, thanks for sharing that. So, what specifically makes you uncertain about continuing in business? If you can tell me a bit more about what you're feeling unsure about, we can figure out what options might suit you better.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “Uh, I feel like I don't know much of business.”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Sure, that makes sense. So maybe you're feeling a bit disconnected from the subject. Let's start by figuring out what parts of your studies you do enjoy or find interesting. Are there any topics or courses that have caught your attention more than others?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “And now I'm sure for my future in this area.”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Alright, that’s understandable. Let’s explore that a bit more. Can you tell me if there’s a particular career path you’ve considered, or maybe something outside of business that you’re curious about?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “Just I want to know just can I change my degree? Could you help me understand my options?”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Absolutely. So, changing your degree is definitely something we can talk through. Let’s start by looking at what alternative programs interest you the most. Are there any specific fields or areas of study that you think you might want to explore instead of business?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “Maybe IT is good. Do you think I needed more work experience before applying?”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Sure, that sounds like a good direction. So for IT, experience can help, but it’s not always a must before you start. Let’s look at the course structure first. Are you interested in seeing how the IT program compares to what you’re doing now?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “Yeah, just show me, please.”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Of course! So let me give you a quick rundown of the IT course structure. Typically, you’ll start with some foundational programming classes and maybe a bit of networking. Now, do you have any specific questions about how that might fit with your future career plans?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “What’s different between a diploma and a master course?”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Sure, good question! So a diploma is usually shorter and more practical, while a master's is more advanced and takes longer. Now, would you prefer a shorter program or are you thinking of going for a full master’s degree down the line?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “I'm not sure what types of jobs are common for people in the master's degree.”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Absolutely, that’s a fair thing to wonder about. With a master’s in IT, you could aim for more specialized or senior roles, like systems analyst or IT project manager. Now, do you think you'd prefer that kind of advanced role, or are you leaning more towards getting into the workforce sooner with a shorter qualification?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “I don't know. How can I stay motivated if I'm not sure about my goals yet?”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Absolutely, it's normal to feel that way. I’d say just focus on exploring a little bit at a time and see what excites you. So, maybe we can set a short-term step for now: would you like to try an introductory IT course just to see how it feels before making a bigger decision?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “After diploma short course and then I can just jump into master degree or I have to do full master degree again.”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Absolutely, that’s a good plan. So yes, often you can start with a diploma or a shorter course, and then later on those credits might count towards a master's. Now, do you feel comfortable starting with that shorter program first to see how it fits you?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “Yeah, I think this sounds great.”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Perfect! That sounds like a solid way to move forward. So let's note that down as your next step. Is there anything else you'd like to ask or any other detail you want to go over before we wrap up?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “Actually, I got some offering from job, so how can I decide between doing further study or starting work after graduation?”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Sure, that's a common dilemma. I’d say it really depends on whether you’re more eager to get hands-on experience or if you’d like to deepen your knowledge first. So, what do you think suits your personality better: jumping straight into work or continuing with a bit more study?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “I'm not sure.”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Absolutely, it’s okay to be unsure. We can take it step by step. So let’s just say for now you explore a little of both. Maybe do an internship to get a taste of work and see how that feels. Does that sound like a plan for you?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “Yeah, sounds great. So, why did I just go for an internship? I should consider it based on my strengths.”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Sure, absolutely. So let’s think about your strengths. If you're good at problem-solving or you enjoy working with technology, look for an internship that lets you use those skills. Now, is there any particular area within IT or business that you think plays to your strengths that we should focus on for that internship search?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “Yeah, I can make a code.”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Great, that sounds like a plan then! So we’ll aim to find you an internship that matches those strengths, and you can see how it feels from there. Is there anything else on your mind before we wrap this up?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “No, all good. Thanks for your time.”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Of course, happy to help! Best of luck with your plans, and feel free to reach out any time you need more advice. Take care!
